plan.contextChunk:
$\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{E}^\mathsf{T}$.

#### Epipolar lines: The game

In order to build an intuition of how epipolar lines work we propose to
play a game. @fig-epipolarlinesgame shows a set of camera pairs and a
set of epipolar lines. Can you guess what camera pair goes with what set
of epipolar lines?


:::{.column-margin}
Answer for the game in @fig-epipolarlinesgame: A-2, B-5, C-4,
    D-6, E-3, F-1.
:::

![Epipolar game. The figure shows six camera pair arrangements, and six sets of epipolar lines. Can you identify which camera pairs correspond to each set of epipolar lines?](figures/3d_scene_understanding/epipolar_game_play.png){width="100%" #fig-epipolarlinesgame}

To solve the game, visualize mentally the cameras and think about how
rays from the origin of one camera will be seen from the other camera.
The epipole corresponds to the location where one camera sees the origin
of the other camera.

Observe that most of the sets of epipolar lines shown in the figure are
symmetric across the two cameras, but it is not always true. What
conditions are needed to have symmetric epipolar lines across both
cameras? What camera arrangements will break the symmetry? If you were
to place two cameras in random locations, do you expect to see symmetric
epipolar lines?

It is also useful to use the equations defining the epipolar lines and
deduce the equations for the epipolar lines for some of the special

elements: Six pairs of simple outlined pinhole cameras, each colored red and blue, arranged in space with visible frusta (viewing pyramids)
Black point at the center of projection (pinhole) for each camera in pairs B, C, D, E, F
Six pairs of epipolar line diagrams, each set in a 2D image plane square (one red, one blue per pair)
Epipoles as convergence points for lines in epipolar line diagrams
Thin lines representing epipolar lines radiating or converging to epipoles or arranged parallel/horizontally/vertically in the squares
Labeled identifiers: capital letters (A-F) for camera pairs, numbers (1-6) for epipolar line pairs

interactions.label: Camera pair (A-F)
interactions.teaches: Switch between different canonical camera configurations to observe how pair geometry changes the epipolar geometry.

interactions.label: Show epipolar lines
interactions.teaches: Toggle visibility of epipolar lines, highlighting their geometric connection to both cameras.

interactions.label: Highlight epipoles
interactions.teaches: Emphasize the focal points—epipoles—where all epipolar lines intersect, clarifying their roles for each camera configuration.

demo_steps.title: Start with Parallel Cameras
demo_steps.narration: Let's start with the simplest case: two cameras side by side, looking straight ahead and perfectly parallel. With this configuration, the epipolar lines in both images are straight and parallel—they don't converge at a point. You can see this corresponds to camera pair A and epipolar lines set 2, which are simple vertical lines. This symmetry is because the cameras see each other's centers at infinity.

demo_steps.title: Observe Skewed Camera Arrangement
demo_steps.narration: Now, let's explore a case where the cameras are no longer symmetrical. For camera pair B (slider position 1), notice how the epipolar lines radiate from a point inside the image for one camera, and from outside for the other. These points are the epipoles. When you enable epipole highlighting, these focal points become clear. The line geometry becomes asymmetric because the cameras don't face the same direction, disrupting the previous symmetry.

demo_steps.title: Extreme Convergence: Epipoles Inside Frame
demo_steps.narration: Let's examine camera pair F by moving the slider to position 5. In this arrangement, the cameras both face toward each other, and the epipoles appear inside both image planes. All epipolar lines visibly converge on these points, creating a dramatic radiating effect. Highlighting the epipoles, you can clearly see how the geometry is now fully focused rather than parallel. This illustrates how the mutual orientation of the cameras directly shapes the epipolar geometry.

demo_steps.title: Hide and Compare Epipolar Geometry
demo_steps.narration: Finally, try turning off the epipolar lines for this configuration. Notice how the visual scene is just two cameras in space—with no way to understand their correspondence relationships until you turn the lines back on. This highlights why epipolar geometry is so essential for matching points between camera views: the lines provide the crucial geometric links.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Initial viewpoint should show both 3D camera pairs and their corresponding epipolar line grids head-on, large enough to compare their spatial relationships, with slight perspective for depth.
interactionPlan.notes: Use thin lines for epipolar lines, and accentuate epipoles as highlighted points or small disks when highlightEpipoles is enabled. Use color-coding (red/blue) consistently between camera models and their epipolar line sets. Arrange UI so that when the camera pair is changed, the corresponding epipolar grid updates side-by-side. If possible, use semi-transparent frusta for clarity.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Rendered image plane pairs (diagrams 1-6) have soft tints not present in the source figure.
Camera pair positions and scales are generally faithful but have slightly different orientation and proportions compared to the source.
Pinhole centers are correctly black for B-F but sometimes seem visually less prominent than in the original.
Amber highlight rectangles are not present in the original figure.
All elements are depicted in 3D and appear slightly raised compared to the original's strict 2D style.
evaluation.notes: Very faithful and clear interactive; minor deviations include additional visual effects and slight geometry/orientation mismatches.
action_items: Remove tinted transparent fills from image planes to match the clean look of the original.
Minimize the thickness and z-offset of highlight rectangles so they do not distract or appear dimensional.
Refine camera wireframe orientation and proportions for maximal closeness to the textbook geometry.
Increase pinhole dot visual prominence, especially for B-F.
If possible, render all elements strictly 2D (no shading or z-offset/sheet effects).

feedback.rationale: The action items all focus on visual styling, precise geometry, and removing unintended effects, indicating the concept is correct but rendering requires execution fixes.
action_items: Remove tinted transparent fills from image planes to match the clean look of the original.
Minimize the thickness and z-offset of highlight rectangles so they do not distract or appear dimensional.
Refine camera wireframe orientation and proportions for maximal closeness to the textbook geometry.
Increase pinhole dot visual prominence, especially for B-F.
If possible, render all elements strictly 2D (no shading or z-offset/sheet effects).

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Diagram numbers and pair letters (1-6, A-F) in the rendered figure are larger and bolder than in the source.
Epipolar lines in the lower diagrams sometimes diverge in detail and count from the very precisely radial or parallel arrangements in the source image.
Black dots representing epipole/pinhole locations are larger than in the original and appear above the cameras, not inside them.
Camera outlines and shapes are approximated, but proportions and perspective sometimes differ from the reference figure.
The color shade for cameras (red and blue) is lighter and more translucent than the original's uniform lines.
evaluation.notes: All elements and interactions are present and work well, though the scale, some proportions, and color match are imperfect.
action_items: Adjust the line color and fill of cameras to match the original's crisper, higher-opacity lines (no translucent fills).
Refine the proportions and placement for the pinhole/epipole dots so they sit more naturally and are of correct size, possibly inside the triangles.
Review the epipolar line patterns for each diagram to be closer to the source in density and angular span, especially for cases 1, 3, and 5.
Reduce the font weight and opacity for number and letter labels for a subtler appearance.
Fine-tune the camera polygon shapes and their placement to better match the reference geometry.

feedback.rationale: All conceptual elements are correct and present; only visual, geometric, and fidelity improvements to the rendered output are required.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Generated figure uses thick polygon camera icons while the source shows thin wireframe pyramids.
The control UI, overlays, and halos do not appear in the original static textbook figure.
Epipole markers/yellow circles are not present in the original image.
Bold drop-shadowed A-F and 1-6 labels are larger and heavier than in reference.
Camera layouts and geometry proportions are approximated but do not exactly match the original’s layout/alignment.
evaluation.notes: All elements and concepts are represented with strong, clear interactions, but the camera and label styling departs noticeably from the minimal wireframe style of the original.
action_items: Refine camera drawing to closer match the thin wireframe pyramid style seen in the textbook, rather than the thick-edged polygons.
Remove epipole highlight circles (yellow overlays) unless explicitly requested, as they are not present in the original figure.
Reduce UI/control overlays and on-canvas halos to more closely mirror the original static schematic nature.
Adjust text labels (A-F, 1-6) for size and opacity to match the delicate typesetting of the source image.
Tweak geometric alignment and spacing in the camera and epipolar line groups to improve layout faithfulness.

feedback.rationale: The action items target visual, styling, and geometric fidelity rather than conceptual or structural issues, indicating the generation needs refinement to better match the original's appearance.
